title: Measurement and Control of the Complex Berry Phase in a Quantum System
authors: Pratik J. Barge, Qian Cao, Niklas Hörnedal, Aurélia Chenu, Kater W. Murch
abstract: The Berry phase is a geometric phase acquired during adiabatic evolution over a closed loop in parameter space. It plays an essential role in geometric quantum gates and other phase-based protocols. In non-Hermitian systems, the Berry phase is complex, introducing fundamentally new geometric effects, including state amplification. In this work, we report experimental measurement of both the real and imaginary components of a Berry phase in a fully quantum system using a superconducting transmon circuit with engineered dissipation. We also demonstrate the path-dependent effects of the imaginary part on the dissipation and its utility in the implementation of non-unitary quantum control. These findings establish a clear geometric distinction between the real and imaginary components of the Berry phase and experimentally confirm the unique adiabatic behavior of non-Hermitian quantum systems.
summary: This paper reports the experimental measurement of both the real and imaginary components of the complex Berry phase using a superconducting transmon circuit. It demonstrates that the imaginary part can be used to implement non-unitary quantum gates that selectively amplify or attenuate quantum states. The findings provide a clear distinction between the two components and confirm the unique adiabatic behavior of non-Hermitian systems.
main_problem: The measurement and control of the complex Berry phase in non-Hermitian quantum systems, specifically distinguishing between its real and imaginary components and utilizing the imaginary part for non-unitary quantum control.
main_result: The researchers experimentally demonstrated that the Berry phase in non-Hermitian systems is complex-valued, where the real part acts as a phase shift and the imaginary part enables state-dependent amplification or attenuation, even implementing a non-unitary geometric gate.
method: The study uses an interferometric protocol with two loops (C+ and C-) and a pi-pulse to cancel dynamical phases for the real part, and measures changes in state norm (survival probability) to isolate the imaginary part, all implemented on a superconducting circuit.
model_or_system: A superconducting transmon circuit with engineered dissipation, modeled by an effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian in a three-level manifold where post-selection on no-jump trajectories creates non-Hermitian dynamics.
key_observables: Real and imaginary components of the Berry phase, expectation values of Sx and Sy, survival probability, population redistribution, and the time-dependent z-component of the state.
important_parameters: Detuning (Delta), drive amplitude (J), decay rate (Gamma), loop duration (T), and the proximity to the Exceptional Point (EP).
main_assumptions: The system relies on the validity of post-selection to maintain the non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian and assumes the adiabatic limit can be approached via shortcuts to adiabaticity.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and parameter space loops; Fig 2 presents tomographic measurements of the real phase; Fig 5 illustrates the input-output relation for the non-unitary geometric gate; Fig 6 shows the tuning of the imaginary phase; Fig 8 compares experimental z-component evolution with theory.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the non-Hermitian Berry phase problem, describes the superconducting platform and Hamiltonian, details the measurement protocols for real and imaginary parts, demonstrates non-unitary gate implementation, analyzes the breakdown of adiabaticity, and concludes with the relationship between the imaginary phase and state tracking.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a concrete experimental realization of complex geometric phases and demonstrates how dissipation can be harnessed for non-unitary quantum control and geometric filtering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) An effective non-Hermitian system is realized through post-selection on trajectories with no quantum jumps from |e⟩to |g⟩at decay rate Γ, while the |e⟩–|f⟩manifold is co- herently driven with amplitude J and detuning ∆. To enable high-fidelity state readout, population in |e⟩is transferred to a neighboring qubit using a parametric SWAP operation. (b) Closed parameter-space loops C± traced in {Jx, Jy, ∆} dur- ing the geometric evolution

FIG. 2. Measurement of the real geometric phase: (a) Tomographic measurement ⟨ψη′,η| ˆSx|ψη′,η⟩and ⟨ˆSy⟩at T = 3 µs and ∆/2π = 3 MHz. (b), (c) and (d) extracted real part of the geometric phase θ(r) +,η with ∆/2π = 3, 1.34, 0.81 MHz. Solid and dashed lines represent theory while markers are experimental data. Opposing loop directions (C+ and C−) result in sign reversal, while repeating the same loop twice with an intermediate swap (η′ = η) cancels both geometric and dynamical phases.

FIG. 3. Measurement of the imaginary part of Berry phase: (a) Postselection success probability decay p±,η with initial state |R+⟩(dashed) and |R−⟩(solid) after one loop in direction η. (b) Ratio of postselection success probabil- ity obtained with eigenstates |R+⟩(dashed) and |R−⟩(solid) as initial states. This eliminates contribution of the dynam- ical phase. (c) Measured imaginary Berry phase (filled cir- cles/squares) starting with either of the two eigenstates, com- pared with theoretical predictions for three different detun- ings. FIG. 4. Time dynamics of survival probability p±,Cη for both eigenstates under different control loop di- rections. (a, b) Survival probability p±,Cη versus time for eigenstates |R−⟩and |R+⟩, respectively, showing decay under control loops C+ (blue), C−(red), and idle conditions (black). (c, d) Corresponding Bloch sphere trajectories for each eigen- state under loops C+ and C−. These trajectories are shown in the undriven frame where |R−⟩→|f⟩and |R+⟩→|e⟩. The black dot represents the idle case initial state.
